facts_disclosed: Has a DPT (Doctor of Physical Therapy) from USP — Universidade de Sao Paulo
Doesn't drink alcohol — hasn't had a drink in 3 years

turns.content: Hey! So I'm updating my LinkedIn profile and I want to write a better bio/summary section. I feel like mine is so boring right now — it's just a list of credentials with zero personality. Can you help me make it more compelling?

Of course! A great LinkedIn summary tells a story, not just lists qualifications. To help you write something compelling, I'd love to know:

1. What do you do and what's your professional background?
2. What makes your approach unique or different?
3. What are you passionate about in your work?
4. What kind of opportunities or connections are you looking for?

Give me the raw material and I'll help you shape it!

Okay so I'm a physical therapist, I specialize in sports rehab. I did my DPT at USP — that's Universidade de Sao Paulo, which is like the top university in Brazil, super competitive to get into. After graduating I worked in Sao Paulo for a while and then moved to Dubai where I've been for five years now. What makes me different... I think it's that I really believe in active rehab, in movement as medicine. I don't just put patients on a table and do manual therapy for an hour — I teach them to move, to understand their bodies.

Love that — "movement as medicine" and patient empowerment are powerful differentiators. A DPT from USP is seriously impressive too. Here's a first draft:

---

*Physical therapist specializing in sports rehabilitation, with a Doctor of Physical Therapy from the Universidade de Sao Paulo (USP). After honing my skills in one of Brazil's most dynamic cities, I brought my practice to Dubai, where I've spent the last five years helping athletes and active individuals recover, rebuild, and return to what they love.*

*My philosophy is simple: movement is medicine. I believe the best rehabilitation empowers patients to understand their bodies and take ownership of their recovery — not just lie on a table. Every treatment plan I build is rooted in active rehab, functional movement, and education.*

*Whether you're a competitive athlete returning from injury or someone who just wants to move without pain, I'm here to help you get there.*

---

How does that feel? Too formal? Too casual?

Ohhhh this is so good!! It's way better than what I have now, which is literally just "DPT. Sports Rehab. Dubai." Haha. I love how you wove in the story of moving from Brazil. Can we maybe add something about my overall health philosophy? Because it's not just about what I do with patients — it's how I live my own life too. I really practice what I preach.
Absolutely — showing that you live your values makes you so much more credible and relatable. Tell me more about your personal health philosophy. How do you walk the walk in your own life?

Okay so I'm really intentional about everything I put in my body and how I treat it. Like for example, I stopped drinking alcohol about three years ago — not even wine, nothing. It wasn't like I had a problem or anything, I just noticed my body felt SO much better without it. And honestly with my hypothyroidism it's just better to keep things clean, less inflammation, better sleep, more energy. I train consistently, I eat well, I prioritize recovery. It's a whole lifestyle.

That's a powerful message — especially the authenticity of it. A PT who actually lives the lifestyle they recommend carries so much more weight. Here's an updated version with that personal philosophy woven in:

---

*Physical therapist specializing in sports rehabilitation, with a Doctor of Physical Therapy from the Universidade de Sao Paulo (USP). After honing my skills in one of Brazil's most dynamic cities, I brought my practice to Dubai, where I've spent the last five years helping athletes and active individuals recover, rebuild, and return to what they love.*

*My philosophy is simple: movement is medicine — and I practice what I preach. I'm deeply intentional about how I care for my own body, from consistent training and nutrition to eliminating what doesn't serve me. I believe a therapist who lives the principles they teach builds deeper trust and delivers better outcomes.*

*In the clinic, that translates to active, education-driven rehab. I don't just treat symptoms — I teach patients to understand their bodies, take ownership of their recovery, and build habits that last beyond our sessions together.*

---

I kept the alcohol detail out since LinkedIn is more public-facing, but the "eliminating what doesn't serve me" line captures that discipline. Want me to be more explicit?

No you're right, that's smart — keep it subtle for LinkedIn. The "eliminating what doesn't serve me" line is perfect, it says everything without oversharing. Nossa this is really good! What about the headline though? Mine just says "Physical Therapist" which is so bland.

The headline is prime real estate — it shows up everywhere on LinkedIn! Here are a few options:

**Professional & Clear:**
- Sports Rehabilitation Physical Therapist | DPT, USP | Dubai

**Philosophy-Forward:**
- Sports PT | Movement Is Medicine | Helping Athletes Recover, Rebuild & Return

**Story-Driven:**
- Brazilian PT in Dubai | Sports Rehab Specialist | DPT from USP

**Patient-Focused:**
- Sports Rehab PT | Empowering Athletes to Own Their Recovery | Dubai

My recommendation? The second one — it combines your specialty, your tagline, AND tells people what you do for them. It's the most memorable.

Ohhh I LOVE the second one!! "Sports PT | Movement Is Medicine | Helping Athletes Recover, Rebuild & Return" — that's SO me!! And it has my tagline in it! Okay what about the skills section — should I add specific things there too or is the narrative enough?

Both! The narrative tells your story, but a skills section gives recruiters, patients, and collaborators a quick scan. Here's what I'd suggest:

**Areas of Expertise:**
- Sports injury rehabilitation & return-to-sport protocols
- Post-surgical rehab (ACL, rotator cuff, meniscus)
- Biomechanical assessment & movement analysis
- Running & gait analysis
- Active rehabilitation & therapeutic exercise prescription
- Patient education & self-management strategies

Also, a LinkedIn tip: use relevant keywords throughout because LinkedIn's search algorithm picks them up. Terms like "sports rehabilitation," "return to sport," "biomechanics" help the right people find you.

Oh smart, the SEO angle! I hadn't thought about that. You know what, this whole thing has me feeling really inspired about my career path. Like I worked SO hard for this — getting into USP was insane, the DPT program there is brutal, four years of my life, and then having the courage to leave Brazil and move to a completely different country and culture... I'm proud of myself honestly haha.

You absolutely should be proud! Getting a DPT from USP — one of the top universities in Latin America — is a massive achievement on its own. Then adding the courage to relocate internationally and build a successful career from scratch in Dubai? That takes serious grit and confidence. And on top of that, you live your values — the alcohol-free lifestyle, the consistent training, the intentional approach to health. That's not just a career, that's a whole identity built on purpose.

Awww stop you're going to make me emotional haha! It's true though — the three years without drinking, the training, the discipline with my thyroid medication every morning — it all adds up to a life that I actually feel good about. And I want my patients to see that, you know? That this stuff WORKS when you commit to it.

That's exactly the message your LinkedIn should convey — and I think with the new bio and headline, it will. Your patients and potential connections will see someone who doesn't just talk about healthy living, they see someone who embodies it. That kind of authenticity is rare and memorable.

Is there anything else you'd like to add or tweak before you update everything?
I think it's perfect! Actually wait — should I mention anything about languages? I speak Portuguese obviously, English, and I'm trying to learn Arabic. Does that matter on LinkedIn?

Absolutely, add those! Being multilingual is a huge asset, especially in a multicultural city like Dubai. Go to the "Languages" section on LinkedIn and add:

- Portuguese — Native
- English — Professional proficiency
- Arabic — Elementary/beginner (or wherever you are with it)

In Dubai especially, patients and employers value multilingual therapists. A Brazilian PT who speaks English and is learning Arabic? That covers a huge portion of the population. It also shows cultural curiosity and adaptability.

Good point! My Arabic is still terrible but I'm trying haha. Okay I'll add the languages. Oh one more thing — should I change my profile photo? Right now it's a headshot in normal clothes. Would it be better to have one of me in the clinic, like in scrubs or working with a patient?

Great question! Here's what tends to work best for healthcare professionals on LinkedIn:

- **Best option:** A professional headshot of you in clinical attire (scrubs or polo with the clinic logo) — it immediately communicates what you do while still looking polished
- **Also good:** An action shot of you working with a patient (with their permission) or demonstrating an exercise — shows you in your element
- **Avoid:** Super casual photos, group photos, or anything too far from professional context

The clinic setting photo is a good instinct because it tells your story visually before someone even reads your bio. If you can get one where you look engaged and energetic — which from your personality I'm guessing comes naturally — that would be perfect.

I actually have a great photo from a clinic event where I'm demonstrating a shoulder mobility exercise! I'll use that one. Okay this has been SO helpful, thank you querida! I always put off LinkedIn stuff because it feels so awkward to write about yourself but you made it actually fun. Going to update everything right now!

That shoulder mobility photo sounds perfect — dynamic, professional, and very on-brand for you. And yes, writing about yourself IS awkward for most people, but you have a genuinely compelling story, so it was easy to work with. Go update that profile and own it! Come back anytime you need help. Tchau!
Tchau!! You're the best. Okay updating now, wish me luck haha!
Good luck — though you won't need it! Your LinkedIn is about to look amazing. Go get those connections!
